name: Integer Operations and Absolute Value
whatIsIt: Now we calculate WITH negatives. ADD/SUBTRACT: move right for +, left for −, across zero. Subtracting a negative adds (5 − (−3) = 5 + 3 = 8). MULTIPLY/DIVIDE sign rules: same signs → positive, different signs → negative ((−4)×(−2)=8; (−4)×3=−12). ABSOLUTE VALUE |n| is the distance from zero, always positive: |−7| = 7.
whyNeeded: Temperature changes, money owed and repaid, scores that go up and down — all need integer arithmetic. The sign rules and absolute value are the backbone of algebra, coordinates and physics.
realLifeUses: Temperature dropping and rising across zero
Golf or game scores that go below zero
Height above/below sea level and how far from sea level (absolute value)

vocabulary.meaning: The distance from zero, always positive: |−7| = 7.
Same signs multiply/divide to +, different signs to −.

story.title: The Bank Balance
story.text: Zara's account shows −₹200 (she owes 200). She deposits ₹500. 'What's the balance now?' asks Uncle. She starts at −200 and moves 500 right: −200 + 500 = 300. 'Plus ₹300!' Then Uncle asks how far from zero −200 is. 'The absolute value — 200,' says Zara.
story.extractedProblem: Balance −₹200, then a ₹500 deposit. What is the new balance?

visual.caption: Absolute value = distance from zero (always positive). Add = move right, subtract = move left.

teachingGallery.title: Signs and distances
teachingGallery.note: Same signs → +, different → −. |n| is distance from 0.

examples.caption: Two negatives multiply to a positive.

standardMethod.summary: Add/subtract by moving on the number line (subtracting a negative adds). Multiply/divide using the sign rule. Absolute value is distance from zero.
steps: Add a positive → move right; subtract → move left. Subtracting a negative ADDS.
Multiply/divide SIGNS: same signs → positive; different signs → negative.
Work out the digits normally, then attach the correct sign.
Absolute value |n|: drop the sign — it's the distance from zero, always positive.
Start at −6 on the number line.

commonMistakes.fix: Same signs → POSITIVE; different signs → NEGATIVE. (−4)×(−3)=+12; (−4)×3=−12.
Absolute value is a DISTANCE, always positive: |−7| = 7.

questions.q: Multiplying two numbers with DIFFERENT signs gives a…

teachBackPrompt: Use a number line and a real 'up and down' example (temperature or a bank balance). Show a grown-up how you add and subtract across zero, and explain the sign rule for multiplying — same signs positive, different signs negative — plus what absolute value means.

revisionCard.summary: Add → move right, subtract → move left; subtracting a negative ADDS. Multiply/divide: same signs → +, different signs → −. Absolute value |n| = distance from zero, always positive (|−7| = 7).

rememberIt.hook: Same signs -> add and keep the sign. Different signs -> subtract and keep the bigger one's sign
rememberIt.unpack: For multiplying: same signs -> +, different signs -> -. (+ x + = +, - x - = +, + x - = -).

studentQuestions.a: Different signs: subtract (7 - 3 = 4) and keep the bigger number's sign (7 is negative) -> -4.

studentQuestions.q: Why is negative times negative positive?
studentQuestions.a: Flipping a flip returns to the start. Removing a debt again and again builds a gain.
